data.title: Taiwan Semi's results, guidance seen as positive for smartphones, AI, autos: BofA
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor's (NYSE:TSM) stronger-than-expected results and guidance are positive for a number of areas in technology, including smartphones, artificial intelligence and automotive-related semiconductors, Bank of America said. 

Revenue attributed to high performance compute growth was flat sequentially, compared to expectations of a 1% decline. Smartphones grew 19% sequentially, above consensus of 14%, though that figure was down 22% of the average seasonal growth. And with Taiwan Semiconductor saying there was no concern of pre-builds, that's seen as positive for Skyworks Solutions (SWKS), Qorvo (QRVO) and Logitech (OTCQB:LOGN), Bank of America analysts said in a note to clients. 

Additionally, growth for internet of things was “robust,” at 20% sequentially, a touch below the 21% estimate. And while Digital Consumer Electronics declined 20% sequentially, it was still better than the 31% decline that analysts were estimating.

Bank of America analysts also noted that Taiwan Semi is working to increase chip-on-a-wafer substrate capacity in 2026, referring to capacity as still “tight.” That bodes well for Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD) and Broadcom (AVGO), Bank of America analysts added. 

Lastly, automotive sales exceeded expectations, up 18% sequentially, well above the 7% estimate. That's seen as a positive for NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) and STMicroelectronics (STM), the investment firm's analysts explained.

“Auto grew above seasonal in 2Q/3Q, surprising given [the] weak Auto backdrop,” the analysts wrote. “We suspect a raise in wafer pricing inflated sales in 3Q and explains recent pick-up in Auto MCU ASP.”

data.title: Prices for AT&T's home internet plans to go up by $5
data.description:
U.S. telecom AT&T (NYSE:T) said Friday it is raising prices for its home internet plans by $5 per month, according to a notice sent to customers by email.

The $5 price hike comes for a third consecutive year, which will be effective on December 1. The company's home internet plans currently start from $55 per month.

The move is expected to provide some cushion against rising expenses and offset costs stemming from the purchase of wireless spectrum owned by EchoStar (SATS) and the buyout of Lumen's (LUMN) consumer fiber business. 

The hike will not be applicable to customers who signed up in the last year or those who joined through the Access from AT&T program for low-income households, the company said.

"As we work to meet the evolving needs of our business and manage increasing operational costs, we're adjusting our internet plan rates to help maintain the high-quality service our customers expect," AT&T spokesperson Jim Kimberly said in a statement.

data.title: American Express raises 2025 revenue growth outlook to 9–10% amid premium card momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Express (AXP) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO Stephen Squeri highlighted a "very strong quarter with revenues up 11% year-over-year to a record $18.4 billion and earnings per share up 19% to $4.14." Card member spending accelerated, with "particularly strong retail spending and a bounce back in travel," alongside "excellent" credit performance. Squeri announced an upward revision in full-year guidance, now expecting "full year revenue growth of 9% to 10% and EPS between $15.20 and $15.50."
  • Squeri noted that "the big news in the quarter was the launch of our refreshed U.S. Consumer and Business Platinum cards," stating "initial customer demand and engagement are exceeding our expectations" and calling it "the strongest start we've seen for U.S. Platinum card refresh." He emphasized the strategic importance of regular product refreshes, citing over 200 globally since 2019, and highlighted that the Consumer and Business Platinum franchise accounts for approximately $530 billion of annual spend globally.
  • Squeri explained the company's coverage expansion, saying "We've grown the number of Amex accepting merchants by nearly 5x since 2017," and detailed the virtuous cycle of engagement, merchant attraction, and reinvestment fueling growth.
  • CFO Christophe Le Caillec stated, "Revenue growth accelerated to 11% this quarter with broad-based growth across revenue lines. Annual card fees are now approaching $10 billion annually and have grown at double digits for 29 consecutive quarters." Le Caillec added, "EPS growth was 19% this quarter with an ROE of 36%."

Outlook

  • Management raised its full-year guidance, now expecting "revenue growth of 9% to 10% and earnings per share between $15.20 and $15.50," assuming a stable macroeconomic outlook through year-end. Le Caillec reiterated, "we are on track for full year revenue growth of 9% to 10%."
  • The company expects card fee growth to "continue to moderate before we see an inflection upward in 2026 as a result of our product refreshes," with the impact of the new Platinum annual fee "peaking 12 months following the effective date."

Financial Results

  • Squeri reported revenues for the quarter at $18.4 billion and EPS at $4.14, both showing significant year-over-year increases. Le Caillec noted that "total spend was up 8.5% FX adjusted, about 2 percentage points higher than Q2," with retail spending up 12% and restaurant spending up 9%. Premium T&E bookings saw "front-of-cabin airline tickets up 14%."
  • The company acquired 3.2 million new cards in the quarter, with over 70% on fee-paying products. Millennials and Gen Z now account for 36% of total spend.
  • Net card fees rose 17% FX adjusted, and net interest income was up 12%. Overall provision expense was $1.3 billion, including a $125 million reserve build. The company returned $2.9 billion of capital to shareholders.

Q&A

  • Sanjay Sakhrani, KBW: Asked about the sustainability of spending strength and potential for further improvement. Squeri responded that "I don't see anything in the horizon here that would indicate that billings are going to slow down or decline," adding that "we're still in a relatively stable environment."
  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about the financial impact of the Platinum refresh on card fees and VCEs. Le Caillec replied, "the short answer is no" impact on mid-teens EPS growth ambition, noting a "material investment" but that "the year is really playing out as we were expecting it to play out."
  • Mark DeVries, Deutsche Bank: Asked about the Platinum refresh's contribution to billed business acceleration. Le Caillec said, "the impact is small" in aggregate, though some partners saw "a 2x increase in terms of the number of customers."
  • Donald Fandetti, Wells Fargo: Sought clarity on SME growth. Squeri noted "good acquisition" and said, "we feel good about acquisitions" and early indications from the business Platinum launch, stating "hopefully, the downturn that we saw from an organic perspective is going to be behind us."
  • Craig Maurer, FT Partners: Asked about competitive takeaways and international strength. Squeri said, "it's a little bit too early to tell" on competitive takeaways, but international growth was "very, very strong," with three of five top countries growing by 18% or more.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was generally positive, with multiple expressions of appreciation for disclosures and questions focused on sustainability and growth levers. The tone was inquisitive but not critical.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in both prepared remarks and responses, supporting their outlook with performance figures and strategic rationale. Squeri emphasized stability and strong positioning, while Le Caillec provided detailed explanations on financial dynamics.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone among both management and analysts was slightly more positive, reflecting stronger reported results and the successful Platinum refresh. Direct quotes such as Squeri's "we're still in a relatively stable environment" and Le Caillec's "we are on track" underscored this confidence.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a guidance raise, from previously reaffirmed targets of 8%-10% revenue growth and $15-$15.50 EPS to 9%-10% and $15.20-$15.50, respectively.
  • Management's tone shifted from cautious confidence to a more assured outlook, citing record results and strong demand for premium products. Analysts maintained focus on competitive dynamics and product refreshes, but questions were more forward-looking given the momentum.
  • Key metric improvement included acceleration in revenue and spend growth, with retail, restaurant, and international spend all showing notable increases over the prior quarter.
  • Strategic focus remained on premium customer engagement, digital innovation, and global merchant expansion, with the successful Platinum refresh as a centerpiece.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted "uncertainty in the environment" but did not indicate any specific emerging risks impacting current performance.
  • Le Caillec explained that card fee growth "will continue to moderate before we see an inflection upward in 2026," outlining the lag between benefit delivery and fee realization as a modeling consideration.
  • Analysts raised questions about competitive pressure, SME growth stability, and the expense curve related to product refreshes, which management addressed by emphasizing strong acquisition, engagement, and prudent forecasting.

Final Takeaway

American Express management highlighted a record-setting quarter with robust revenue and earnings growth, driven by strong spending, especially among premium customers and younger cohorts. The company raised its full-year revenue and EPS guidance, citing the successful launch of the refreshed Platinum cards, broad-based international growth, and exceptional credit quality. Management expressed confidence in the sustainability of these trends and their ability to deliver on long-term growth aspirations, while continuing to invest in premium value propositions and digital innovation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street searches for direction as investors navigate worries on regional banks' health
data.description:
U.S. stocks were mixed on Friday, as Wall Street was rattled by concerns on the health of regional banks and credit risks while the U.S. government shutdown entered its 17th day.

The major indexes seesawed throughout the morning trade. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.2%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.3%.

“Markets are now paying attention to credit, and there are likely more skeletons in the closet of lenders. I am not sure yet whether this poses systemic risk, but the gaping jaw between business development companies and the S&P 500 will close one way or another,” said Chester Ntonifor, chief strategist at BCA Research, via X.com.

Stocks of investment bank Jefferies Financial (JEF) +4.7%, regional banks Zions Bancorporation (ZION) +3.1%, and Western Alliance (WAL) +0.7% were initially lower on Friday as investors worried about the quality of loans and investments made by the firms, but have since recovered. Goldman Sachs (GS) and J.P. Morgan (JPM), however, are some of the top decliners in the Dow (DJI).

The KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index (KRX) closed down 6.3% on Thursday trading, its biggest drop since April, following the disclosures by two banks of irregularities related to some loans -- last +1%.

Meanwhile, the U.S. government shutdown continued to grind on as it entered its third week, after a GOP funding bill failed for the 10th time. Prediction market Kalshi now sees the government shutdown lasting almost 40 days, the longest in history.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.00%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.47%.

“The bond market is pricing in 2 more 25 bps rate cuts by year-end and 3 more 25 bps cuts in 2026. That would bring the Fed Funds Rate below 3%. After a brief hiatus, easy money is back,” said Charlie Bilello, chief market strategist at Creative Planning.

Wall Street's major averages closed lower on Thursday amid increased volatility due to the ongoing U.S.-China trade conflict and bad loan reports in banks, while investors continued to assess earnings reports and the ongoing U.S. government shutdown.

data.title: Eli Lilly falls on orforglipron FDA voucher exclusion, Trump on weight-loss meds
data.description:
Eli Lilly is trading down 3% in late Friday morning trading after the pharma did not receive a National Priority Review voucher for its oral GLP-1 asset, orforglipron, and President Trump made remarks about reducing the price of a rival obesity treatment.

On Thursday, the Commissioner's National Priority Voucher pilot program awarded nine vouchers to drugs considered to either fill an unmet medical need, address a public health crisis, promote domestic manufacturing, or lead to cheaper costs. The scheme cuts review time to 1-2 months from the usual 10-12. 

Also, during a press conference in the Oval Office to announce price cuts for in vitro fertilization drugs, Trump hinted that he is eyeing discounts for Novo Nordisk's (NVO) Ozempic (semaglutide), approved for type 2 diabetes but used off-label as a weight loss therapy.

Calling Ozempic the "fat-loss drug," the president said that its price could be lowered to $150 a month. Novo currently offers Ozempic, and its weight-loss approved drug Wegovy, for $499 a month for cash-paying customers through its NovoCare pharmacy.

CMS Administrator Mehmet Oz, however, noted that negotiations to lower the price of Ozempic and other weight-loss drugs is still ongoing.

Street analysts appeared to be more concerned with the potential price reduction for Ozempic and other obesity treatments than the orforglipron voucher snub.

JP Morgan's Chris Schott, who rates Lilly overweight, said that it is unclear whether Trump's comments on Ozempic are related to his "Most Favored Nation" pricing program or Inflation Reduction Act pricing negotiations. Still, he said that investors are expecting Ozempic to eventually be priced at $150-$200 a month.

Cantor's Carter Gould noted that Lilly's share price decline may be the result that investors were expecting the negotiated price of semaglutide under the IRA to be in the mid-$200s range. He also has Lilly at overweight.

"We continue to view any ultimate negotiated Most Favored Nation (MFN) price as more of a headline risk versus a true fundamental change to Lilly and Novo's  businesses," added BMO Capital Markets' Evan David Seigerman, who rates Lilly outperform.

data.title: Bank stocks retrace some of Thursday's losses as investors' nerves settle
data.description:
U.S. bank stocks started the climb back up on Friday after suffering a jolt down on Thursday, as bank commentary and analysts point out that banks' fundamentals appear to be generally solid, and the instances of credit risk events aren't representative of the broader sector.

The SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF (NYSEARCA:KRE) rose 1.1% in late morning trading, stabilizing from its 6.2% slump on Thursday. One of the biggest gains came from stocks that sank the most in the previous session. Zions Bancorporation (NASDAQ:ZION) gained 3.1% after Baird upgraded the stock, saying its selloff was "overdone."

Though not a regional bank, Jefferies Financial (NYSE:JEF), which slid 11% on Thursday over concerns related to potential losses from its investment in bankrupt First Brands, jumped 5.0% on Friday. That's after Oppenheimer upgraded the stock to Outperform.

Other movers include: Banc of California (BANC) +2.1%, Western Alliance Bancorporation (NYSE:WAL) +1.2%, Flagstar Financial (FLG) +0.8%, and First Horizon (FHN) +1.1%.

Some regional banks, whose stocks may be moving more on Q3 earnings include: Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) +1.0%, Regions Financial (RF) -0.3%, Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) +2.0%, Comerica (CMA) +2.2%, and Bank OZK (OZK) -2.4%.

Citi analyst Andrew Hollenhorst points out that the disclosures of issues with certain loans at two regional banks and bankruptcies in the private credit market, on their own, don't amount to much from a macroeconomic perspective. "But the concern is not about the known losses, it is about what may be still unknown," he wrote in a note to clients. "In other words, risk premia are rising from low levels."

Across the industry, bank commentary has been generally positive, with the glaring exceptions of disclosures from Zions  Bancorporation (NASDAQ:ZION) and Western Alliance  Bancorporation (NYSE:WAL) regarding suspected fraud related to a few loans. 

"I think one of the biggest surprises is that we’re only talking about three credits," when banks deal with "thousands upon thousands of credits on a daily basis," 22V's Bill Hebel wrote in a note. The concerns, though, focus on: the complexity of banks' investments and whether they're conducting sufficient due diligence; growth in loans to non-depository financial institutions, which can lack transparency; and the potential for high severity of loss from alleged fraud.

The last point "is the most concerning trend among the three credits," Hebel said. "While credits move to non-performing all the time, it’s rare to see multiple instances of alleged fraud." That, along with the losses from the First Brands and Tricolor collapses, are likely to make investors "much more circumspect."

Hebel noted that banks' earnings so far have seen pretax preprovision profit-driven beats "across the board" on solid net interest income, and, in many cases, strong fee income from capital markets/trust/wealth management. At the same time, consumer and commercial real estate credit have been improving.

"We think as we move through the rest of earnings, we’ll see more stability in the group and investors picking spots in favorite names," he wrote.

The sector slump can have broader indications, as other sectors followed suit on Thursday. A selloff in risk assets would reinforce Citi's expectation of 25-basis-point rate cuts at each of the next four Federal Open Market Committee meetings. "The cuts we envision are based on evidence the labor market is softening, but a sell-off in risk assets would make cuts more likely," Hollenhorst said.

data.title: Novo taps U.S. pharma veteran from AbbVie amid drug pricing scrutiny
data.description:
A seasoned pharmaceutical executive from AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV) will soon become the new head of corporate affairs at Novo Nordisk (NVO) as the weight loss drugmaker faces renewed scrutiny from the Trump administration over its drug pricing strategy.  

Greg Miley, a senior vice president of government affairs at AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV), announced in a LinkedIn post on Friday that he will be joining the Danish pharma giant as its Senior Vice President, Corporate Affairs.

“This opportunity comes at a pivotal moment for the company, and I am eager to get started and contribute to our mission of improving the lives of people living with chronic diseases,” said Miley, who has previously held senior executive roles at companies such as Sanofi (SNY) and Pfizer (PFE).

As part of the appointment, Miley said he will relocate to Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) home country, Denmark, and that he will handle the company’s global communication and public affairs initiatives.

The announcement came hours after President Donald Trump claimed that the price of Novo’s (NVO) popular weight loss/diabetes therapy Ozempic should be “much lower” once his administration's negotiations with the company conclude. Shares of the pharma giant fell ~4% on the news.

At an event in the Oval Office on Thursday, Trump said that the blockbuster injectable, which comes with a list price of roughly $1,000 and is sold directly by Novo (NVO) for $499 per month for cash-paying customers, could be available at $150 a month.

Eli Lilly (LLY), which sells similar GLP-1 drugs for weight loss and diabetes at a list price of over $1,000 per month, also traded lower in reaction.

data.title: 31 out of 36 companies beat earnings estimates this week: Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
U.S. stocks opened mixed on Friday, as investor anxiety mounted over the financial health of regional banks, fueled by rising credit risk concerns.

Thursday’s session ended on a sour note, with major indexes closing lower amid heightened volatility. Investors reacted to negative developments, including ongoing tensions in the U.S.-China trade dispute, troubling loan data from several banks, and continued analysis of corporate earnings. 

The prolonged U.S. government shutdown continues to weigh on market confidence as it stretches into a third week, adding further uncertainty for investors.

Earnings Roundup: 

Out of the 36 companies that reported earnings this week, 31 of them beat EPS estimates and surpassed revenue expectations. 

Let’s take a look at some of the companies that reported earnings this week. 

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) nudged up its guidance for full-year net interest income and introduced guidance for 2026. Q3 earnings reflected strong results across all business lines, but showed particularly robust performance in Markets, fueled by demand for financing, and Asset & Wealth Management on higher client activity.

The company now expects 2025 net interest income, excluding markets, of ~$92.2B vs. its prior guidance of ~$92B.

Wells Fargo (WFC)’s Q3 earnings topped the Wall Street consensus in a strong showing for the first results since the Federal Reserve lifted its asset cap on the bank.

The bank expects 2025 net interest income to be “roughly in line” with 2024’s $47.7B, unchanged from its prior guidance and compared with the Visible Alpha consensus of $47.5B. Noninterest expense is expected to be ~$54.6B, up from its previous guidance of ~$54.2B.

Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) Q3 expenses exceeded the average analyst estimate. Meanwhile, earnings, revenue, and net interest income came in higher than Wall Street consensus estimates, helped by increased investment banking activity and growth in its assets and wealth management unit.

Q3 GAAP EPS of $12.25, topping the average analyst estimate of $11.09, rose from $10.91 in Q2 and $8.40 in last year’s Q3.

Total revenue of $15.2B, beating the $14.3B consensus, increased from $14.6B in the prior quarter and $12.7B a year ago.

Citigroup (NYSE:C) raised its full-year guidance for net interest income after the bank delivered a double-beat on third-quarter headline results, driven by strength across the board.

The lender now sees FY25 NII, ex-markets, advancing ~5.5%, with expectations for continued fee momentum across the Investment Banking, Services and Wealth units. 

Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) kicked off the healthcare earnings season, reporting better-than-expected results for Q3 2025 and raising its full-year sales outlook.

Concurrently, J&J announced plans to separate its orthopedics division as a standalone unit named DePuy Synthes within 18–24 months in a bid to fast-track its MedTech portfolio’s shift toward higher-growth and higher-margin markets.

Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) Q3 2025 results slightly fell short of Street forecasts for revenue, and the company narrowed its full-year earnings outlook.

North Chicago, Illinois-based Abbott recorded $11.4B in revenue for the quarter with ~7% YoY growth on a reported basis, missing the consensus by $20M as its diagnostic segment continued to contract.

Earnings next week: 

Several companies are scheduled to report earnings next week, such as Steel Dynamics (STLD), Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF), and Preferred Bank (PFBC) reporting on Monday, with Netflix (NFLX), Coca-Cola (KO), Philip Morris (PM), 3M (MMM), Lockheed Martin (LMT), and General Motors (GM) reporting on Tuesday. 

Wednesday will see earnings reports from Tesla (TSLA), IBM (IBM), and AT&T (T), while Thursday will include T-Mobile US (TMUS), Blackstone (BX), Intel (INTC), and Honeywell (HON). P&G (PG) and General Dynamics (GD) will report earnings on Friday.

data.title: BHP's China Jimblebar iron ore stocks rise as contract talks drag on - Reuters
data.description:
Jimblebar iron ore stockpiles supplied by BHP (NYSE:BHP) are piling up at major Chinese ports to the highest levels in three months, Reuters reported Friday, as the price dispute between the company and China's state-run iron ore buyer risks dragging on for months.

BHP (NYSE:BHP) owns and operates the Jimblebar mine in Western Australia, and China Mineral Resources Group reportedly told steel mills and traders last month not to buy BHP's (BHP) Jimblebar fines, and as a result, inventories of Jimblebar fines at some Chinese ports have jumped to ~2.6 million metric tons as of October 14, the highest level since July.

BHP (BHP) and CMRG reportedly are still negotiating their 2026 term contract, although BHP told Reuters it is "in commercial negotiations [and] not aware of an overall BHP product ban."

Jimblebar fines inventories at the Caofeidian port alone, among China's busiest for handling iron ore, surged 26% from the end of September to 800,000 tons as of October 13, according to the report.

The temporary squeeze in Jimblebar fines supply has not yet supported prices much, as the cargo could be substituted by other products such as Rio Tinto's (RIO) flagship Pilbara fines product, according to the report.

Jimblebar fines are a type of medium-grade cargo that mills typically use to make sinter ore, which is then processed into hot metal for crude steel.

data.title: Bank OZK sells part of defunct Chicago development for $84M
data.description:
Bank OZK (NASDAQ:OZK) closed the sale of the northern part of Chicago's failed Lincoln Yards project in Chicago to JDL Development, as expected, for $84M, the regional lender disclosed in a recent filing.

The company initially provided a $128M loan for the large Chicago land tract in 2019, but assumed ownership in March 2025 after delays and funding problems derailed prior developer Sterling Bay's plans, Bloomberg reported.

The remaining $78.6M in foreclosed assets, as of Sept. 30, 2025, comprises three Real Estate Specialties Group ("RESG") assets, the largest two of which -- land in Los Angeles and an office property in Boston -- were under contract to sell, OZK said in a quarterly Securities and Exchange Commission filing earlier in the week.

OZK's total foreclosed assets roughly halved from $160M in Q2 and ticked up from $78M in the year-ago period.

"An important element of our business model is to structure loans so that sponsors/capital partners are highly incentivized to support the loans, even in challenging times," OZK said in the 10-Q. It does so by obtaining sizable equity stakes relative to its low leverage loans and lending on strong assets, keeping sponsors and capital partners invested even if projects face delays. 

"Despite the challenging macroeconomic environment, we have had only a small number of RESG substandard credits and foreclosed assets over the past 13 quarters," OZK said.

The Thursday filing also showed that OZK's Q3 earnings rose both Q/Q and Y/Y, topping expectations, despite a second straight month of record net interest income.

OZK shares extended losses from the prior session, sliding 3.3% in Friday late morning trading, underperforming most peers intraday. The stock is on track to close out the week with an 8.5% decline, amid broader weakness across regional banks stemming from credit risk jitters.
